reference: GATE CS — Computer Organization and Architecture / Memory and I/O

map.condition: Compute overall access time with hit/miss rates across cache levels
Number of cache sets / lines for a set-associative or direct-mapped cache
Effective access time with TLB and page faults
Address bus bits needed for word-addressable memory

formulas.name: AMAT — Single-Level Cache
formulas.whenToUse: One level of cache; h = hit rate, t_c = cache access time, t_m = main-memory access time.

terms.means: cache hit rate (fraction of accesses found in cache)
main memory access time (full penalty on miss)

formulas.trap: Confuse simultaneous-access model (penalty = t_m) with sequential model (penalty = t_c + t_m); read the problem carefully.

formulas.name: AMAT — Sequential Miss Penalty Model
formulas.whenToUse: Cache is always checked first (time t_c consumed for every access), then main memory is accessed only on a miss.

terms.means: additional main-memory penalty paid only on a miss

formulas.trap: This model adds t_c unconditionally; the simultaneous model does not — mixing them is the #1 GATE mistake.

formulas.name: AMAT — Two-Level Cache (L1 + L2 + Main Memory)
formulas.whenToUse: Two-level cache hierarchy; L1 miss leads to L2 lookup, L2 miss leads to main memory.

terms.means: local hit rates of L1 and L2 cache respectively
access times for L1 and L2 cache

formulas.trap: h_2 here is the LOCAL hit rate of L2 (hits in L2 / accesses reaching L2), not the global hit rate.

formulas.name: AMAT — Miss-Rate × Penalty Form
formulas.whenToUse: Textbook form used when miss penalty (not total main-memory time) is given separately.

terms.means: extra cycles paid on a miss

formulas.trap: Miss penalty is the EXTRA time beyond t_hit; do not double-count t_hit.

formulas.name: Number of Sets in Set-Associative Cache
formulas.whenToUse: Finding number of sets given cache size C, block size B, and k-way associativity.

terms.means: number of ways (associativity); k=1 → direct-mapped, k=S → fully associative

formulas.trap: Direct-mapped is 1-way set-associative (k=1, S=C/B); fully associative has exactly 1 set (S=1).

formulas.name: Physical Address Breakdown for Cache
formulas.plain: Address = [tag (N-s-b bits)] [set index (s bits)] [block offset (b bits)]
formulas.whenToUse: Determining tag, index, and offset field widths from address size N, number of sets S=2^s, block size B=2^b.
formulas.trap: For direct-mapped cache s = log2(number of lines), not log2(cache size); for fully associative s = 0.

formulas.name: Number of Cache Lines (Direct-Mapped)
formulas.whenToUse: Counting total lines/blocks in a direct-mapped cache.
formulas.trap: L is also the number of sets for direct-mapped cache; do not confuse lines with words.

formulas.name: Direct-Mapped Line Assignment
formulas.whenToUse: Determining which cache line a main-memory block maps to in a direct-mapped cache.
formulas.trap: Two blocks with different content can map to the same line → cache conflict miss (thrashing).

formulas.name: Cache Hit Ratio
formulas.whenToUse: Computing hit rate from a trace or given access pattern statistics.

terms.means: number of accesses found in cache
number of accesses not found in cache

formulas.trap: Compulsory (cold) misses are unavoidable; a higher associativity reduces conflict misses but not compulsory misses.

formulas.name: Write Policies — Write-Through vs Write-Back
formulas.latex: \text{Write-Through: write to cache AND memory simultaneously}\\\text{Write-Back: write to cache only; memory updated on eviction (dirty bit)}
formulas.plain: Write-Through: every write goes to cache + memory. Write-Back: write to cache; memory updated only when line is evicted.
formulas.whenToUse: Cache coherence / bandwidth questions about how writes are handled.

terms.means: flag indicating a Write-Back line has been modified and must be written back

formulas.trap: Write-Through needs a write buffer to avoid stalls; Write-Back reduces bus traffic but complicates coherence.

formulas.name: Cache Replacement Policies
formulas.plain: LRU >= FIFO >= Random in typical hit-rate (LRU is optimal for temporal locality)
formulas.whenToUse: Choosing or comparing replacement algorithms in cache-miss trace problems.

terms.means: Least Recently Used — evicts line not used for longest time
First-In First-Out — evicts oldest loaded line
Optimal (Belady) — evicts line used furthest in future; theoretical lower bound

formulas.trap: FIFO suffers Belady's anomaly (more frames → more misses for some reference strings); LRU does not.

formulas.name: Address Bus Width
formulas.plain: Address bits = log2(Memory size / Word size)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding minimum address bus width for a word-addressable memory system.

terms.means: total addressable memory in bytes (e.g. 4 GB = 2^32 bytes)
bytes per addressable word (e.g. 2 bytes for 16-bit word)

formulas.trap: Byte-addressable memory uses all bits; word-addressable memory needs log2(size/word_bytes) fewer bits.

formulas.name: Interleaved Memory Bandwidth
formulas.whenToUse: Computing peak bandwidth of n interleaved memory banks, each w bytes wide, with cycle time t_cycle.

terms.means: width of each bank in bytes
memory cycle (access + restore) time per bank

formulas.trap: Peak bandwidth requires block transfer aligned to bank boundaries; wrap-around interleaving may reduce effective bandwidth.

formulas.name: Low-Order vs High-Order Interleaving
formulas.plain: Low-order: bank = address mod n (consecutive words in consecutive banks). High-order: bank = floor(address/B) mod n (consecutive words in same bank).
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about interleaved memory organisation; low-order is used for burst/cache-fill transfers.

terms.means: block size (bytes per bank-group in high-order)

formulas.trap: Low-order interleaving maps CONSECUTIVE words to DIFFERENT banks; high-order maps a contiguous block to ONE bank.

formulas.name: Cache-Block Fill Time with Interleaving
formulas.whenToUse: Time to fill a cache block of k words from interleaved banks after the first word is fetched.

terms.means: time to get first word (memory access + bus transfer)
number of words in cache block
bus cycle time for one word transfer

formulas.trap: Subsequent words can overlap with bank cycle time in a pipelined memory; count only bus cycles for words 2…k.

formulas.name: Effective Memory Access Time with TLB
formulas.whenToUse: No page faults; TLB hit bypasses page table, TLB miss requires one extra memory access for page table.
formulas.trap: On a TLB miss the sequence is: TLB + page_table_in_memory + actual_data = t_TLB + 2*t_m (not just t_m).

formulas.name: Effective Access Time with Page Faults
formulas.whenToUse: Including page-fault probability p; T_fault includes disk access, page replacement, and restart.

terms.means: page-fault rate (fraction of accesses causing a page fault)
effective access time without a fault (e.g. TLB + memory)
total time for a page-fault service (disk seek + transfer + restart)

formulas.trap: T_fault is typically 10^6–10^8 ns; even p = 10^{-6} can double T_eff — show the arithmetic in GATE answers.

formulas.name: Page Table Size
formulas.plain: PT size = (Virtual address space / Page size) * PTE size
formulas.whenToUse: Finding memory consumed by the page table for a process.

terms.means: bytes per page-table entry (holds frame number + flags)

formulas.trap: Page table lives in main memory; multi-level paging reduces space by not allocating unused sub-tables.

formulas.name: Virtual Address Breakdown (Single-Level Paging)
formulas.whenToUse: Splitting a virtual address into page number and offset given virtual address size V bits and page size 2^p bytes.

terms.means: virtual page number — index into page table

formulas.trap: Physical address size may differ from virtual address size; frame number bits = physical address bits - p.

formulas.name: Two-Level Page Table Address Breakdown
formulas.plain: VA = [p1 bits] [p2 bits] [offset d bits]; accesses = t_TLB + 3*t_m on TLB miss
formulas.whenToUse: Two-level (hierarchical) paging questions; determines number of page-table levels accessed on a miss.
formulas.trap: A TLB miss in a 2-level scheme needs 2 memory reads (L1 directory + L2 table) + 1 for data = 3 total memory accesses.

formulas.name: TLB Access Sequence Summary
formulas.latex: \text{TLB hit:}\; t_{TLB}+t_m \qquad \text{TLB miss (1-level):}\; t_{TLB}+2t_m \qquad \text{TLB miss (2-level):}\; t_{TLB}+3t_m
formulas.plain: TLB hit: t_TLB + t_m. TLB miss (1-level paging): t_TLB + 2*t_m. TLB miss (2-level paging): t_TLB + 3*t_m.
formulas.whenToUse: Quick reference for computing access latency depending on TLB outcome and paging level.
formulas.trap: Each level of page-table hierarchy adds one t_m on a TLB miss; always add t_m at the end for actual data fetch.
